coredata.dc:title: Semiquantitative analysis of carbon quantum dot-labeled exosomes based on carrier ampholyte-free paper-based isoelectric focusing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chenyu
Yu, Haoran
Zhang, Lingyi
Zhang, Weibing
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a semiquantitative analysis method based on carrier ampholyte-free paper-based isoelectric focusing (CAF-PAD-IEF) was developed for the isolation and detection of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) labeled exosomes. A stable pH gradient was established on the glass fiber paper by the migration of H+ and OH−. Three pH gradients of pH 3–9, 3–7 and 6–9 were established by changing the species and pH of anolytes and catholytes. The pH gradients were evaluated with phycocyanin (PC), ovalbumin and bovine hemoglobin (BHb) as standard proteins, and egg white was used as model sample too. The linear correlation coefficient (R2) of the pH gradient (pH 3–9) reached 0.9966. Exosomes obtained by ultracentrifugation from urine were used as the standard to realize the semiquantitative detection of exosomes. The urine exosomes were labeled with CQDs and then successfully focused based on their isoelectric points (pIs). The exosome concentration was determined semiquantitatively according to the fluorescence intensity of the focused bands. The standard curve was drawn based on the fluorescence intensity peak areas of the focused bands with the R2 value of 0.9881. This method was applied to the determination of exosome concentration in fetal bovine serum (FBS) and concentrated urine with the relative errors of −4.17 % and −8.70 % respectively compared with the values determined by nanoparticle tracking analysis (NTA), showing great potential in quick semiquantitative determination of exosomes in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Exosome
Paper-based isoelectric focusing
Carrier electrolyte-free
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CQDs fluorescent labeling procedure of exosomes
Principle of pH gradient formation on CAF-PAD-IEF
Formation of pH gradient and validation by the standard proteins
Separation of the proteins in the actual samples
IEF separation of exosomes by CAF-PAD-IEF

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Overall diagram and (b) actual photo of the CAF-PAD-IEF device. (c) Schematic diagram of principle of CAF-PAD-IEF: 1, electrophoresis power supply; 2, anode reservoir; 3, cathode reservoir; 4, paper substrate fluid channel.

The actual pH gradients curves formed by CAF-PAD-IEF system and measured with pH test paper: (a) pH 3–7; (b) pH 6–9; (c) pH 3–9. The distance mark line in Fig. 2 was used to align the color picking position. (d) Calibration curve of the pIs vs. focusing positions in the pH gradient of 3–9. (Applied voltage: 1.0 kV). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The separation results of the FITC-labeled egg white proteins and the mapping to the theoretical pH curve (Applied voltage: 0.9 kV). (b) Diagram of paper segmentation. (c) MALDI-TOF MS spectra of egg white proteins (1: lysozyme; 2: ovomucoid; 3: ovalbumin. Applied voltage: 1.0 kV).
(a) TEM images of exosomes. CAF-PAD-IEF analysis for exosomes with pH ranges of (b) pH 3–9 (Applied voltage: 1.0 kV); (c) pH 3–7 (Applied voltage: 0.8 kV); (d) pH 6–9 (Applied voltage: 0.8 kV).

The isoelectric focusing results of exosomes in (a) FBS and (b) concentrated urine. (c) Semiquantitative calibration curve of exosomes. (d) Semiquantitative analytical results of exosomes in FBS and concentrated urine. The error bars in the figure represent the standard deviation of three parallel experiments. (Applied voltage: 1.0 kV).

head.ce:title: Semiquantitative analysis of carbon quantum dot-labeled exosomes based on carrier ampholyte-free paper-based isoelectric focusing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a semiquantitative analysis method based on carrier ampholyte-free paper-based isoelectric focusing (CAF-PAD-IEF) was developed for the isolation and detection of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) labeled exosomes. A stable pH gradient was established on the glass fiber paper by the migration of H+ and OH−. Three pH gradients of pH 3–9, 3–7 and 6–9 were established by changing the species and pH of anolytes and catholytes. The pH gradients were evaluated with phycocyanin (PC), ovalbumin and bovine hemoglobin (BHb) as standard proteins, and egg white was used as model sample too. The linear correlation coefficient (R2) of the pH gradient (pH 3–9) reached 0.9966. Exosomes obtained by ultracentrifugation from urine were used as the standard to realize the semiquantitative detection of exosomes. The urine exosomes were labeled with CQDs and then successfully focused based on their isoelectric points (pIs). The exosome concentration was determined semiquantitatively according to the fluorescence intensity of the focused bands. The standard curve was drawn based on the fluorescence intensity peak areas of the focused bands with the R2 value of 0.9881. This method was applied to the determination of exosome concentration in fetal bovine serum (FBS) and concentrated urine with the relative errors of −4.17 % and −8.70 % respectively compared with the values determined by nanoparticle tracking analysis (NTA), showing great potential in quick semiquantitative determination of exosomes in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Paper-based analysis devices (PADs) can transfer substance with the capillarity of fibers, providing a lightweight, environmentally friendly and inexpensive platform for isoelectric focusing (IEF). In 2007, Andres [1] first introduced paper material into the field of microfluidic, which has been widely used in pesticide testing [2], blood analysis [3], urine testing [4], and other aspects. Danchana et al. [5] developed an origami IEF system on the filter paper for the separation and classification of glutamate and histidine. Wu et al. [4] established an ion depletion zone of anion concentration polarization interface in the paper fluid channel to realize the separation and detection of microalbuminuria in urine samples from diabetic patients.

Carrier ampholytes (CAs) are synthetic mixtures of amphoteric components with low molecular weight and high buffering capacity, which are widely used in the IEF process. However, CAs have strong ultraviolet absorption at low wavelengths, thus affecting the sensitivity of UV detection. MS detection was also affected by CAs because of serious impacts on the ionization of target components by increasing the ion current [6,7]. Immobilized pH gradient (IPG) IEF can avoid ion source contamination of MS caused by CAs and enable a reduction of cathodic drift to avoid pH instability [8,9]. However, the preparation process of IPG is complicated. Using the directional migration of H+ and OH− provided by the electrolytes to establish the IEF system is another way to avoid the influence of CAs, indicating characteristics of fast and simple operation and good compatibility with MS. Huang et al. [10] established a capillary isoelectric focusing (cIEF) method without CAs, and successfully separated R-phycoerythrin and green fluorescent protein, hemoglobin and cytochrome C. Wang et al. [11] constructed a free-flow isoelectric focusing system without CAs for isolation of three major proteins: ovomucoid, ovalbumin and ovotransferrin in egg white. Xie et al. [12] developed an IEF method without CAs on paper-based analytical device for the first time, and separated phycocyanin (PC) and bovine hemoglobin (BHb) on it.

Exosomes are nanoscale vesicle structures secreted by the cells. They widely distribute in blood, urine, saliva, etc, playing an important role in the process of cell information transmission and have been applied to diagnosis, drug delivery and treatment for diabetes [13], leukemia [14], angiocardiopathy [15] and so on. The isolation methods for exosomes include ultracentrifugation [16], solvent precipitation [17], size-exclusion chromatography (SEC) [18] and immunoaffinity [19,20], etc. Ultracentrifugation is the most common method that is currently considered as the gold standard in the industry, and has a decent recovery rate compared with other approaches [21]. The quantitative methods for exosomes include nanoparticle tracking analysis (NTA) [22], flow cytomeometer [23], dynamic light scattering [24], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [25], etc. As an effective method to isolate exosomes by pIs, IEF has many advantages such as low energy consumption, fast reaction and good portability [26,27], which has been not only applied to determination of pIs and qualitative analysis of exosomes, but also shows great potential in quantification analysis of exosomes. Yang et al. [28] developed a cIEF method to characterize pIs and surface charge of exosomes, and applied this method to separation of model exosomes derived from normal and cancer cell lines and healthy human serum. Anderson et al. [29] coupled IEF with chromatography tandem mass spectrometry to achieve comprehensive characterization of exosomes from mesenchymal stem cells and their protein content.

In this paper, a carrier ampholyte-free paper-based isoelectric focusing (CAF-PAD-IEF) analysis method was developed, and wide and narrow pH gradients were established stably on the glass fiber paper without using CAs. The feasibility of the established method was verified by standard proteins and model samples. Exosomes were focused on the CAF-PAD-IEF device after fluorescently-labeled by carbon quantum dots (CQDs). And a semiquantitative determination method of exosomes was developed based on fluorescence intensity of focused exosomes band, providing a theoretical basis for the quantitative analysis of exosomes in real biological sample.

The CAF-PAD-IEF device includes four parts: electrophoresis power supply, anode reservoir, cathode reservoir and paper substrate fluid channel. The overall schematic diagram of the device is shown in Fig. 1 . The glass fiber paper with pore diameter of 0.22 μm and thickness of 0.25 mm was cut into small strips (45.0 mm × 3.0 mm) as the paper substrate. Two 1.5 mL centrifuge tube covers were used as reservoirs. Platinum wires (0.5 mm × 10 mm) were used as electrodes.

The two reservoirs were fixed 40.0 mm apart, and then a 45.0 × 3.0 mm paper strip was placed between them with both ends (2.5 mm) immersed in the solution of reservoirs. Glycine (Gly)-HCl solution (10 mmol/L, containing 0.2 % hydroxyethylcellulose (HEC), pH 3) and Gly-NaOH solution (10 mmol/L, pH 9) were loaded to two reservoirs as anolyte and catholyte respectively to establish a pH 3–9 gradient. Then the two electrodes were immersed in these electrolytes and electric field was applied to this conductive loop. Soak the paper substrate in deionized water and the matrix solution in sequence to moisten the surface before applying electric voltage. After the current was stable, 5.0 μL sample solution was loaded to the middle of the paper substrate. During the process of IEF, the focusing process of the colored protein bands and the current change were recorded. The current would increase and then decrease to a stable value slowly, which is regarded as the end of focusing. This current value is generally in the range of 30–60 μA depending on the types and concentrations of electrolytes in the system and the experimental environment. The anolyte and catholyte for establishing pH 6–9 gradient are HAc-NH4Ac (10 mmol/L, containing 0.2 % HEC, pH 6) and NH4OH–NH4Ac (10 mmol/L, pH 9) respectively. For pH gradient of 3–7, they are HAc-HCl (10 mmol/L, containing 0.2 % HEC, pH 3) and NH4OH–NH4Ac (10 mmol/L, pH 7) respectively.

40 μL FITC fluorescein was added to 2.000 mL protein solution. The solution was centrifuged to obtain the supernatant. Then the solution was incubated under room temperature for 1 h. After 4 °C overnight, FITC labeled fluorescent protein solution was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs fluorescent labeling procedure of exosomes
ce:section.ce:para: The exosome solution (10 μL) was incubated with CQDs (10 μL) dissolved in PBS solution (0.01 mol/L, 4.000 mL) at 37 °C for 24 h, then transferred to an ultrafiltration tube with the molecular weight cut-off of 100 kDa. The solution was centrifuged at 3000 g for 10 min and washed three times to remove the unbound CQDs. The 0.45 μm filter membrane was used before loading to paper substrate.

The exosomes isolated by ultracentrifugation were labeled by CQDs and then diluted to different concentrations (3.890 × 1011, 2.853 × 1011, 1.902 × 1011, 0.9511 × 1011, 0.3890 × 1011 Particles/mL) according to results of NTA. Then they were focused by the CAF-PAD-IEF device. The focusing results were processed by Image J to obtain the calibration curve of the fluorescence intensity vs. exosomes concentration. The peak area of fluorescence intensity peak was taken as value of fluorescence intensity. Each sample was measured in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of pH gradient formation on CAF-PAD-IEF
ce:section.ce:para: The establishment of stable pH gradient is of great concern to IEF. The schematic diagram of the principle of pH gradient formation on the paper substrate of the CAF-PAD-IEF system is shown in Fig. 1c. When an electric field is applied, H+ and OH− migrate from the anode and cathode reservoirs to the other pole and react on the paper substrate. When the neutralization reaction reaches dynamic equilibrium, the pH gradient is established. When 10 mmol/L HCl and NaOH are used as electrolytes, the pH gradient linearity is poor and cannot focus PC and BHb (Fig. S1) because the pH jump range of the neutralization reaction is from 5.3 to 8.7. Considering strong buffering ability of Gly in this range, it helps to improve the linearity of the pH gradient while keeping good stability. Then 10 mmol/L Gly was added to the sample solution and electrolytes. Taurine was also applied to reduce the baseline interference caused by the matrix [30].

In CAF-PAD-IEF, the focusing process of colored sample can be dynamically monitored by directly observing the color change on the paper-based surface. The color band loaded in the middle of the paper surface would diffuse firstly, then gradually migrate to a specific position and achieve focusing. As to the colorless samples, the focusing process can be visualized through the help of fluorescent labeling. Combined with FITC (Fig. S2), the target protein can present a high contrast fluorescence signal under UV light excitation, thus achieving highly sensitive and real-time tracking. Besides, control experiments were performed. Because FITC is not amphoteric compound, it cannot be focused by IEF. The unreacted FITC was flushed out from the substrate after 5 min of loading, resulting in no influence on the detection of focused proteins (Fig. S3). However, it should be considered that FITC achieves fluorescence labeling by covalently binding to the amino groups in proteins [31]. The binding conditions vary among different proteins and may cause the acidic shift of the pI. It deserves careful consideration in the subsequent development of precise pI determination and fluorescence quantitative methods for proteins.

In IEF, the electroosmotic flow (EOF) may drive the established pH gradient towards the cathode, thereby affecting its stability during the focusing procedure. Therefore, the control of EOF in the IEF process is indispensable. HEC is a commonly used nonionic surfactant that can effectively inhibit EOF. Therefore, 0.2 % HEC was applied to the anolyte and the effect of HEC on EOF was investigated with Rhodamine B as EOF marker in Fig. S4. As we can see, by using 0.2 % HEC as EOF inhibitor, the migration distance of Rhodamine B is close to that of diffusion without applying electric field, indicating significant decrease of EOF.

In addition, what worth consideration is that the most suitable voltage value in the focusing processes varied due to the environment conditions. When the temperature is low and humidity is high, the moisture in the air will increase the conductivity and the voltage needs to be increased. As for high temperature and low humidity, the applied voltage should be appropriately reduced. Therefore, during the research process of this paper, the voltage value applied to the CAF-PAD-IEF device is in the range of 0.8–1.2 kV as shown in the legend of Figures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of pH gradient and validation by the standard proteins
ce:section.ce:para: During the establishment of the pH gradient, different electrolyte species were used to form the gradient of pH 3–9 in the absence of carrier electrolyte, with two colored proteins of PC and BHb as standard proteins. The conductive loop formed by 10 mmol/L pH 3 HAc-HCl, pH 9 NH4OH–NH4Ac (Fig. S5) and pH 3 KH2PO4–Na2HPO4, pH 9 Na2HPO4–H3PO4 (Fig. S6) cannot establish the desired pH gradient, and cannot successfully focus the standard proteins in theoretical positions. However, a uniform and stable pH gradient from 3 to 9 can be established with 10 mmol/L pH 3 Gly-HCl and pH 9 Gly-NaOH as electrolytes (Fig. 2 a). In addition, the stability and shape of the pH gradient was explored by applying electric field for different periods of time (Fig. S7). It can be seen that stability and shape of the pH gradient kept stable within 5 min. However, it is worth noting that the strips would become dry and unable to be powered on after a course of time, which hindered experiments with a power-on time of more than 5 min.

To broaden the application range of the method, three pH gradients, pH 3–9, pH 6–9 and pH 3–7, were established and shown in Fig. 2. Wide and narrow pH ranges can be controlled by employing various electrode liquid solutions with good linearity, meeting the requirements of IEF for different samples. Three proteins, PC (pI 3.4), ovalbumin (pI 4.5, fluorescence labeled by FITC) and BHb (pI 7.2), were used as model samples to determine the linearity of pH 3–9 gradient. Fig. 2d shows that the focusing position of each protein matches the pIs, and the linear correlation coefficient (R2) of the pH gradient curve of the three proteins is 0.9966, indicating good linearity. A mixed solution of PC and BHb were used as a sample to verify the separation effects by MALDI-TOF MS. Fig. S8 shows that PC and BHb are respectively focused on the theoretical pI positions and have no interference with each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of the proteins in the actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: In actual samples, a variety of proteins can migrate and be focused according to their respective pIs in the pH gradient under the action of the electric field, resulting in efficient separation. Proteins in egg white labeled with FITC were used as the actual sample, and the focusing results are shown in Fig. 3 a. Two fluorescent bands were focused on the paper substrate. Based on the theoretical pH curve, the two bands corresponded to ovomucoid with pI of 4.1 and ovalbumin with pI of 4.5. In addition, the established method was also evaluated with milk as the actual sample, and the focusing results of the fluorescent-labeled milk samples are shown in Fig. S9. There are three bands formed, which roughly be correspond to casein (pI 4.6), lactoglobulin (pI 5.2), and lactoferrin (pI 8.7). In conclusion, fluorescent-labeling can show the focusing results of proteins directly and can be applied to real samples. CAF-PAD-IEF can separate and focus proteins in actual samples according to their different pIs. It has the advantages of high speed, simple operation and strong universality, providing a new tool for the analysis of proteins.

The focused proteins of the egg white sample were further verified by MALDI-TOF MS. The paper substrate was cut evenly into four pieces after the focusing (Fig. 3b). As can be seen in Fig. 3c, MALDI-TOF MS can detect three major proteins in egg white, in which ovomucoid (28 kDa, pI 4.1) and ovalbumin (44–54 kDa, pI 4.5) had strongest abundance in segment (1), which are consistent with their theoretical pIs. Lysozyme (14–15 kDa, pI 11.0) were not focused successfully because its pI exceeds the pH range of CAF-PAD-IEF system (pH 3–9). In addition, small amount of lysozyme and ovomucoid can also be detected in all four segments. This may be due to the adsorption of glass fiber paper surface, leading to contamination of high abundance proteins. In the future, other paper substrate with low adsorption or glass fiber modified with polyacrylamide will be used to reduce the adsorption of proteins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IEF separation of exosomes by CAF-PAD-IEF
ce:section.ce:para: Exosomes isolated by ultracentrifugation from urine were used as model exosomes. The results of NTA, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Western Blot all demonstrate the successful isolation of exosomes from urine (Fig. 4 a and Fig. S10). CQDs can be encapsulated by exosomes, thus enabling fluorescent labeling of urine exosomes [32,33]. Then the model exosomes labeled by CQDs were subjected to CAF-PAD-IEF with different pH ranges. Fig. 4b shows the fluorescence intensity distribution map and the corresponding pI value of exosome determined according to the calibration curve (pH 3–9). The fluorescent band is focused at position of pH 6.5, which is consistent with previous investigation [28]. The focusing process was conducted in triplicate with the pH 3–9 gradient, and the calculated RSD was obtained as 1.68 %. The component from the focused band was collected and characterized by Western Blot in Fig. S10. The positive expression of TSG101, CD9 and CD63 verifies successful separation of exosomes by IEF.

The exosomes labeled with CQDs were diluted to 75 %, 50 %, 25 % and 10 % of the original concentration, respectively. After focusing, the peak area of corresponding peak intensity of the fluorescent band was used as quantitative standard to fit the quantitative calibration curve (Fig. 5 c). Although other extracellular vesicles may also be labeled by CQDs, part of them can be separated by pretreatment of ultrafiltration and filtration, and IEF separation can also separate them from CQDs labeled exosomes. Then most fluorescent signals of the focused band are from CQDs labeled exosomes, which can meet the requirement of semiquantitative determination. However, the interference of other extracellular vesicles needs to be further investigated in the future by using CAF-PAD-IEF system with higher resolution. In the concentration range of 0.3890 × 1011 - 3.890 × 1011 Particles/mL, exosome concentration and fluorescence intensity show linear relationship with R2 of 0.9881, meeting the requirements of semiquantitative analysis of exosomes.

The established method was applied to quantify the exosome concentration in FBS and concentrated urine as shown in Fig. 5b. The fluorescent band of exosomes in FBS is focused at the position of pH 6.2 with RSDs of 4.98 % (n = 3). The component of the fluorescent band was collected for Western Blot analysis, showing positive expression of CD9 and TSG101 (Fig. S11). The fluorescent band of exosomes in concentrated urine is focused at pH 6.5 with RSDs of 1.49 % (n = 3), corresponding to the position of model exosomes, and the band shows positive expression of CD9 and TSG101 too (Fig. S11). According to the calibration curve, the testing concentration of exosomes in FBS is 1.15 × 1011 Particles/mL, while the result of NTA is 1.20 × 1011 Particles/mL, showing a relative error of −4.17 %. The testing concentration of exosomes in concentrated urine is 3.99 × 1010 Particles/mL according to the calibration curve, while the result of NTA is 4.37 × 1010 Particles/mL, showing a relative error of −8.70 %. The result can meet the requirements of semiquantitative analysis, and proves that the established fluorescence semiquantitative method can be applied to real samples. The analysis of exosomes is easy to operate and visualize.

A CAF-PAD-IEF method was successfully established and applied to the semiquantitative analysis of exosome samples labeled by CQDs. Stable pH gradients were established on the glass fiber paper without CAs and the linear correlation coefficient (R2) of the pH gradient (pH 3–9) reached 0.9966. This method was applied to the semiquantitative analysis of exosomes in real samples, with a relative error of −4.17 % between the calculated value and the obtained NTA result in FBS and −8.70 % for concentrated urine. The developed method has advantages of easy operation, low consumption and high speed, showing a broad application potential for the analysis and quantification of exosomes.

Chenyu Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Haoran Yu: Methodology. Lingyi Zhang: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Weibing Zhang: Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledged the support of the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JKJ01251717).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2025.128412.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Patterned paper as a platform for inexpensive, Low-Volume, portable bioassays

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.W. Martinez, S.T. Phillips, M.J. Butte, et al. Patterned Paper as a Platform for Inexpensive, Low-Volume, Portable Bioassays. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 46(8) (2007) 1318-1320. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.200603817.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microfluidic paper based analytical devices for the detection of carbamate pesticides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Beshana, A. Hussen, S. Leta, et al. Microfluidic Paper Based Analytical Devices for the Detection of Carbamate Pesticides. Bull. Environ. Contam. Toxicol. 109 (2022) 344-351. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00128-022-03533-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Paper based analytical devices for blood grouping: a comprehensive review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ebrahimi Fana, M. Paknejad, M. Aminian. Paper based analytical devices for blood grouping: a comprehensive review. Biomed. Microdevices 23(3) (2021) 34. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10544-021-00569-w.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous enrichment and separation based on ion concentration polarization effect on a paper based analytical device

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.Y. Wu, Y.H. Wang, B.S. Niu, et al. Simultaneous enrichment and separation based on ion concentration polarization effect on a paper based analytical device. Anal. Chim. Acta 1208 (2022) 339844. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2022.339844.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Separation and fractionation of glutamic acid and histidine via origami isoelectric focusing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Danchana, N. Yamashita, M.I. Umeda, et al. Separation and fractionation of glutamic acid and histidine via origami isoelectric focusing. J. Chromatogr. A. 1706 (2023) 464247. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chroma.2023.464247.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Capillary isoelectric focusing of proteins and peptides using an In-Line cIEF-ESI interface with improved MS characteristics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.M. Kerr, O.L. Schneider, S. Tichy, et al. Capillary Isoelectric Focusing of Proteins and Peptides Using an In-Line cIEF-ESI Interface with Improved MS Characteristics. Anal. Chem. (2025). https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.4c05010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microfabrication of a tapered channel for isoelectric focusing with thermally generated pH gradient

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Huang, J. Pawliszyn. Microfabrication of a tapered channel for isoelectric focusing with thermally generated pH gradient. Electrophoresis 23 (2002) 3504-3510. https://doi.org/10.1002/1522-2683(200210)23:20<3504::AID-ELPS3504>3.0.CO;2-X.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reducing cathodic drift during isoelectric focusing using microscale immobilized pH gradient gels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Lomeli, A.E. Herr. Reducing Cathodic Drift during Isoelectric Focusing Using Microscale Immobilized pH Gradient Gels. Anal. Chem. 96(21) (2024) 8648-8656. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.4c00788.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A porous layer open-tubular capillary column with immobilized pH gradient (PLOT-IPG) for isoelectric focusing of amino acids and proteins

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Liu, S. Cheddah, S. Liu, et al. A porous layer open-tubular capillary column with immobilized pH gradient (PLOT-IPG) for isoelectric focusing of amino acids and proteins. Anal. Chim. Acta 1048 (2019) 204-211.https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.10.036.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Capillary isoelectric focusing without carrier ampholytes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Huang, X.Z. Wu, J. Pawliszyn. Capillary Isoelectric Focusing without Carrier Ampholytes. Anal. Chem. 72(19) (2000) 4758-4761.https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trac.2023.117018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carrier ampholyte-free free-flow isoelectric focusing for separation of protein

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wang, L.Y. Zhang, H.F. Sun, et al. Carrier ampholyte-free free-flow isoelectric focusing for separation of protein. Electrophoresis 40 (2019) 2610-2617.https://doi.org/10.1002/elps.201900148.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carrier ampholyte-free isoelectric focusing on a paper-based analytical device for the fractionation of proteins
